description: A collection of hands focused on optimal play of specific suit combinations, from basic honor management to advanced restricted choice situations.

hands.title: 311. AK Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: AK opposite small. Cash the AK hoping to drop honors, then force out remaining high cards. Basic suit combination play.

hands.title: 312. AQ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: AQ opposite small. Lead toward the AQ from dummy. If West has the King, finesse the Queen. Basic finesse or play for the drop decision.

hands.title: 313. KQ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: KQ opposite small. Must lose the Ace but need to set up the suit. Lead the King to force the Ace. Entry management is critical with no Ace in the suit.

hands.title: 314. AJ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: AJ opposite small. Lead toward the AJ. If West plays low, insert the Jack. Standard finesse position against West for the King and Queen.

hands.title: 315. KJ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: KJ opposite small. Lead toward the KJ hoping to find the Ace or Queen onside. Duck or finesse depending on which honor you need.

hands.title: 316. QJ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: QJ opposite small. Lead toward QJ to force out the Ace and King. Intermediate honor combination establishing length tricks.

hands.title: 317. AQJ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: AQJ opposite small. One high card (King) missing. Lead toward AQJ and finesse. If the King is onside, lose zero tricks in the suit.

hands.title: 318. KQJ Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: KQJ opposite small. Missing the Ace. Lead the King to force out the Ace. Strong sequence makes this straightforward despite missing top honor.

hands.title: 319. AJT Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: AJT opposite small. Lead toward AJT, finesse the Ten. If it loses to the Queen, re-enter dummy and finesse the Jack. Touching intermediates provide two finesse chances.

hands.title: 320. KJT Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: KJT opposite small. Three-card tenace. Lead the King hoping to pin the Ace, then finesse the Jack if needed. Complex three-card tenace requiring careful play.

hands.title: 321. QJT Opposite Small
hands.notes: Spades: QJT opposite small. Intermediate spots. Lead the Queen to force out an honor. Continue with Jack then Ten to establish length winners.

hands.title: 322. AQ Opposite Jxx
hands.notes: Spades: AQ opposite Jxx. Finesse the Queen, then if it wins, lead the Jack from dummy to unblock the suit. The Jack is a useful intermediate card.

hands.title: 323. KQ Opposite Jxx
hands.notes: Spades: KQ opposite Jxx. Missing the Ace with blocked honors. Lead the Jack from dummy first. If West ducks, continue with low toward KQ. Complex entry management.

hands.title: 324. AQJ Opposite xxx
hands.notes: Spades: AQJ opposite xxx. Maximum expected tricks: finesse twice. Lead from dummy, finesse the Jack. Return to dummy, finesse the Queen. Three tricks if King onside.

hands.title: 325. KQJ Opposite xxx
hands.notes: Spades: KQJ opposite xxx. Without the Ace but with length. Lead the King to force the Ace. With dummy entries, establish the remaining honors as winners.

hands.title: 326. AKT Opposite xxx
hands.notes: Spades: AKT opposite xxx. Cash the Ace then lead toward the KT. If the Queen and Jack are split, the Ten becomes a winning finesse card. Intermediates used correctly.

hands.title: 327. Axxx Opposite QJx
hands.notes: Spades: Axxx opposite QJx. Lead the Queen from dummy. If West covers with King, win the Ace and the remaining cards are high. Direction and timing matter.

hands.title: 328. Axxx Opposite KJx
hands.notes: Spades: Axxx opposite KJx. Two lines: finesse the Jack or play Ace then King hoping the Queen drops. Complex two potential lines depending on distribution clues.

hands.title: 329. Axxx Opposite QTx
hands.notes: Spades: Axxx opposite QTx. Lead the Queen from dummy. Spot cards matter: if West covers, win Ace and the Ten is promoted. Restricted choice applies if honors split.

hands.title: 330. Kxxx Opposite QJx
hands.notes: Spades: Kxxx opposite QJx. Missing the Ace, direction-sensitive. Lead the Queen from dummy hoping to pin the Ten or force the Ace from the right side.

hands.title: 331. Axx Opposite QTxx
hands.notes: Spades: Axx opposite QTxx. Five cards opposite three. Lead toward the Queen. If it wins, return and lead the Ten for a second finesse. Length compensates for missing honors.

hands.title: 332. Axx Opposite JTxx
hands.notes: Spades: Axx opposite JTxx. Lead the Jack from dummy. If West covers, win the Ace and the Ten is promoted. Combination play with intermediates.

hands.title: 333. Kxx Opposite JTxx
hands.notes: Spades: Kxx opposite JTxx. Missing Ace and Queen with length. Lead the Jack from dummy. If West plays the Ace, the King and Ten are promoted. If West ducks, the Jack wins.

hands.title: 334. 9-Card Fit Missing the Queen
hands.notes: Nine spades between the hands missing the Queen. With 9 cards, play the Ace and King (percentage play). The Queen drops singleton from East. Eight ever, nine never.

hands.title: 335. 8-Card Fit Missing the Queen
hands.notes: Eight spades between the hands missing the Queen. With 8 cards, finesse for the Queen (eight ever). Cash the Ace, then lead toward KJ and finesse the Jack.

hands.title: 336. 9-Card Fit Missing the King
hands.notes: Nine spades missing the King. Finesse or play for the drop? With 9 cards, the drop is slightly better. But here the finesse works too since West holds Kxx.

hands.title: 337. 9-Card Fit Missing KJ
hands.notes: Nine spades missing KJ. Finesse the Queen; if it wins, re-enter dummy and finesse the Ten for the Jack. With West holding KJx, both finesses succeed.

hands.title: 338. 9-Card Fit Missing QJ
hands.notes: Nine spades missing QJ. Cash the Ace, then finesse the Ten. If West started with QJx, the Ten loses to an honor but the King drops the other. Play for the split.

hands.title: 339. 10-Card Fit with One Honor Missing
hands.notes: Ten spades missing the Queen. With 10 cards, play the Ace or King to drop the singleton or doubleton Queen. Here East has singleton Queen. Always play for the drop with 10 cards.

hands.title: 340. Restricted Choice in Suit Combinations
hands.notes: Play the Ace, East drops the King. By restricted choice, East is more likely to hold a singleton King than KQ or KJ. Finesse the Ten on the next round. West started with QJx.
